one_line_takeaway: A benchmark/survey paper showing that among current digital object-detection attacks OSFD is the strongest and most transferable across CNN detectors but all four benchmarked attacks fail to transfer well to the transformer-based DINO, and that adversarial training on a full (not partial) dataset mixing high-perturbation attacks with complementary objectives (localization-focused OSFD + classification-focused high-epsilon EBAD) gives the most robust YOLOv3/Faster R-CNN detectors.
key_finding: Two headline findings, both from the paper's own experiments. (1) Transferability gap: OSFD is the strongest and most broadly transferable attack, causing mAP drops exceeding 84% on all CNN-based detectors in white-box and black-box settings, but taking 44s/image (vs. 17s for EBAD, 84s for CAA); DINO (transformer-based) is comparatively robust to all four attacks, with mAP drop capped at 27.3% for OSFD and under 5% for the other three -- exposing a cross-architecture (CNN-to-Vision-Transformer) transferability gap that the paper identifies as the field's next frontier. (2) Adversarial training: fine-tuning on 100% adversarial data outperforms mixing with benign images (e.g., OSFD-trained YOLOv3 mAP degrades only from 75.8% to 73.5% between 0% and 100% adversarial training data, while a 25% adversarial mix causes disproportionately large robustness loss, e.g., CAA-trained-model mAP vs. OSFD collapsing from 53.0% to 23.0%). Robustness does not transfer symmetrically: models trained on weaker attacks (CAA, EBAD) remain vulnerable to OSFD (mAP 53.0%/49.4% respectively), but OSFD-trained models generalize well to CAA/EBAD (mAP >65%). Mixing attacks with complementary objectives (spatial/localization via OSFD + semantic/classification via high-epsilon EBAD) produces the most robust detector overall: the OSFD 75%/EBAD-eps50 25% mix reaches 64.8% mAP against EBAD eps=50 (vs. 53.5% for OSFD-only training) while retaining OSFD-level robustness (68.9% mAP vs. OSFD-YOLO).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (beyond its Section 2 survey of prior attacks/defenses) is threefold: (1) a unified benchmark framework for digital, non-patch-based adversarial attacks on object detectors that standardizes detectors (YOLOv3, Faster R-CNN, YOLOX, FCOS, DETR, Mask R-CNN, DINO), datasets (train on COCO, test on VOC2007), and two new metrics -- APloc (mAP with all classes fused into one, isolating localization failure) and CSR, Classification Success Ratio (micro-averaged recall conditioned on correct localization, isolating classification failure) -- plus perceptual-cost metrics (LPIPS, L2) alongside the conventional Lp norms; (2) an empirical cross-architecture transferability study of four selected attacks (OSFD, EBAD, CAA, PhantomSponges) in white-box and black-box settings across the seven detectors; and (3) a systematic adversarial-training study on YOLOv3 and Faster R-CNN (fine-tuned on VOC2007 trainval after COCO pretraining) that compares single-attack training, partial (25/50/75%) adversarial-image mixes with benign data, and mixed-attack training combining attacks with different objectives (e.g., OSFD + EBAD, OSFD + CAA).
threat_model: Digital-only adversarial examples (no physical or patch attacks) against object detectors, evaluated under white-box, grey-box, and black-box attacker knowledge as appropriate to each of the four benchmarked attacks: OSFD (white-box, random-output, feature-manipulation attack), EBAD (grey-box/query-based ensemble attack, used here in a white-box variant against a surrogate), CAA (white-box ensemble/context-aware mislabeling attack using Faster R-CNN + YOLOv3 as surrogates), and PhantomSponges (universal fabrication/inference-latency attack, generated only on YOLOv3). Attacks generated on a surrogate (YOLOv3 or Faster R-CNN) are then transferred black-box to unseen target detectors (DETR, FCOS, YOLOX, DINO, and the non-surrogate of the pair) to measure cross-architecture transferability, notably from CNNs to the transformer-based DINO detector.
limitations: Explicitly scoped to digital, non-patch-based attacks only -- physical and patch attacks are excluded by design because their 'cost' models (patch size, printability, real-world variables) are incompatible with the paper's Lp/LPIPS-based metrics; the authors flag this as future work (Section 5.3). Only 4 of many surveyed attacks are actually benchmarked (object-vanishing category has no representative because no recent vanishing attack had public code). AFOG was excluded after the authors found its effectiveness collapses when the attacked image is resized back to original dimensions (mAP recovers from 13.23% to 66.56% on DETR), which the paper interprets as brittleness rather than a genuine robustness result. Adversarial training experiments use only VOC2007/COCO in-domain data; the authors explicitly note cross-dataset/domain-gap generalization of adversarial training (e.g., to different weather, lighting, sensors) remains untested and open. All 'attacked' robustness numbers reflect a white-box worst case for training-time attack generation; no certified/formal robustness guarantee is provided (that category is only discussed in the related-work survey, not implemented).

datasets_used: PASCAL VOC2007 (trainval for adversarial fine-tuning; test set for all evaluation)
quantitative_results: OSFD causes mAP drops exceeding 84% on all CNN-based detectors tested (white-box and black-box)
OSFD requires ~44s/image to generate vs. 17s for EBAD and 84s for CAA (NVIDIA TITAN RTX)
DINO mAP drop limited to 27.3% under OSFD and under 5% under the other three attacks (CAA, EBAD, PhantomSponges)
EBAD on YOLOv3 causes a 51.0% relative drop in CSR vs. only 19.5% in APloc, reflecting its mislabeling-focused objective
PhantomSponges causes only ~10-11% APloc drop across detectors, consistent with its universal, detection-preserving fabrication objective
AFOG attack: mAP on DETR is 13.23% at attacked-image resolution but rebounds to 66.56% once the image is resized back to original dimensions (L-infinity=30)

TOG attack on YOLOv3 (TOG's own implementation): untargeted mAP drops from 78.72% (benign) to 1.88%, but on the MMDetection reimplementation of YOLOv3, benign mAP is 75.84% and untargeted-attacked mAP is 59.47%, illustrating poor cross-implementation transfer
Adversarial training: OSFD-trained YOLOv3 benign mAP degrades only from 75.8% (0% adversarial data) to 73.5% (100% adversarial data)
25% adversarial-data mix: CAA-trained-model robustness against OSFD (YOLO) collapses from 53.0% mAP (100% adversarial) to 23.0% mAP (25% adversarial)
OSFD-only-trained model vs. EBAD eps=50: 53.5% mAP; OSFD75%/EBAD-eps50-25% mixed training raises this to 64.8% mAP while maintaining 68.9% mAP against OSFD
EBAD-trained-model mAP against CAA eps=30 rises from 38.1% to 66.8% as EBAD's own epsilon increases from 10 to 50, while benign mAP drops from 75.4% to 68.7% (robustness-accuracy trade-off)

Perceptual metrics: OSFD has the lowest L-infinity norm in the benchmark (7.0) yet an LPIPS of 0.19, nearly double EBAD eps=10's LPIPS of 0.11, showing L-infinity is a poor perceptibility proxy; EBAD eps=50 reaches LPIPS 0.62
baselines_compared: Benign fine-tuning (fine-tuned on unattacked VOC2007 images only)
Single-attack adversarial training (CAA, EBAD at multiple epsilon values, OSFD, PhantomSponges)
Mixed-attack adversarial training (OSFD+CAA and OSFD+EBAD combinations at various ratios)
